skill.name: linear
skill.description: Manage Linear issues, projects, comments, labels, and status updates through the Linear GraphQL API.
skill.summary: Use Linear GraphQL workflows from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: 0xSero

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command, credential, network, and keyword patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, GraphQL examples, and ordinary Linear terminology. The confirmed risk is legitimate but sensitive: the skill enables sandboxed bash and full network access to call Linear with LINEAR_API_KEY, so publication should include a credential handling warning.

medium_findings.title: Networked Linear API Requests Use an Environment Secret
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill requires LINEAR_API_KEY and instructs the agent to send it in an Authorization header to the Linear GraphQL API. This is expected for a Linear integration, but it creates credential exposure risk if commands are copied, logged, or redirected to an untrusted endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key, network endpoint, and Authorization header are explicitly documented. The context is a legitimate Linear workflow, so the risk is credential handling rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Sandboxed Bash with Full Network Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill enables bash and full network access so it can issue curl requests. This is required for the documented GraphQL workflow, but it expands the execution surface and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission block and curl instructions directly confirm command execution and network use. The execution permission is marked sandboxed, which reduces but does not remove risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts mostly point to fenced Markdown examples, inline code spans, GraphQL operation names, and JSON examples. No Ruby execution or dynamic shell interpolation is present in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown and GraphQL examples, not executable Ruby source. The only real command example is the documented curl call, covered separately as a medium risk.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Secret File Patterns Are Protective Configuration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The filesystem entry permits reads under .opencode, while the same permission block denies .env, .key, and .secret files. This indicates a constrained configuration rather than an attempt to harvest local secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission block explicitly denies common secret file patterns. Hidden directory access is limited to the tool configuration path shown in the skill metadata.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance detections match ordinary Linear content such as description fields, GraphQL id fields, state names, and issue status terms. No encryption logic, host probing, or network scanning behavior is described.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Linear issue metadata and GraphQL schemas. I found no evidence of cryptographic code, scanning commands, or reconnaissance instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Command Sends Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented curl example sends LINEAR_API_KEY in an Authorization header. This pattern is necessary for Linear API access but can expose secrets if logs or shell history are retained.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and environment variable expansion are directly shown in the curl example. The skill also tells users never to log or expose the key.

dangerous_patterns.title: Full Network Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill metadata grants full network access. This is expected for an API integration, but users should understand that the skill can make outbound requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network permission is explicit in the metadata. No evidence found that the skill targets non-Linear destinations by default.

content.user_title: Manage Linear Issues from AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Teams lose time switching between chat, code, and Linear updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured way to create, update, and query Linear work items.
actual_capabilities: Creates Linear issues through documented GraphQL mutations.
Updates issue status, priority, estimates, and related fields.
Adds comments to existing Linear issues.
Queries viewer, team state, and project status information.
Uses LINEAR_API_KEY and LINEAR_TEAM_ID from the environment.
Reports completed Linear actions with identifier, URL, and changed details.
limitations: Requires a valid Linear API key and team ID before use.
Uses bash and curl rather than a dedicated Linear SDK.
Does not validate custom GraphQL queries beyond Linear API responses.
Should not be used with API keys that have broader access than needed.

use_cases.title: Create Engineering Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn bug reports, implementation notes, or review comments into Linear issues with priority and description fields.

use_cases.title: Update Sprint Progress
use_cases.description: Move issues between Linear states and add comments after code changes, reviews, or deployments.

use_cases.title: Check Project Status
use_cases.description: Query project progress, issue counts, and completion status from the Linear GraphQL API.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Linear issue for this bug. Title it clearly, include the reproduction steps, and set priority to medium.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Status Comment
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a comment to Linear issue ENG-123 summarizing the investigation result and the next action.

prompt_templates.title: Move Work to the Right State
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the available team states, then update issue ENG-123 to the correct in-progress state.

prompt_templates.title: Review Project Progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the project status for the current release and summarize progress, open work, and completed issue count.

output_examples.input: Create a Linear issue for a failed login redirect after password reset.
output_examples.output: Linear Action Completed. Type: create_issue. Identifier: ENG-245. URL: Linear issue link. Details: Created a high-priority bug with reproduction steps and expected behavior.

output_examples.input: Update ENG-245 after the fix is merged.
output_examples.output: Linear Action Completed. Type: update_issue. Identifier: ENG-245. URL: Linear issue link. Details: Moved the issue to Done and added a short merge summary.

output_examples.input: Show the current release project status.

best_practices: Use a Linear API key with the minimum permissions needed for the workflow.
Review generated issue titles, descriptions, and comments before sending them.
Keep issue identifiers and team IDs explicit in prompts when updating existing work.
anti_patterns: Do not paste LINEAR_API_KEY into prompts, issue descriptions, or comments.
Do not point LINEAR_API_URL at an endpoint you do not control or trust.
Do not use this skill for bulk destructive changes without reviewing each mutation.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Linear API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It expects LINEAR_API_KEY and LINEAR_TEAM_ID to be available as environment variables.

faq.question: Can it create new Linear issues?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents the Linear issueCreate GraphQL mutation and required input fields.

faq.question: Can it update issue status?
faq.answer: Yes. It can query team states and use issueUpdate to change issue fields.

faq.question: Does it support project status checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a project status query for state, progress, issue count, and completed issue count.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill needs network access, bash, and an API key. These are legitimate for Linear, but they require careful credential handling.

faq.question: Does the audit show malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No. I found no prompt injection, credential theft intent, hidden exfiltration endpoint, or unsafe file write behavior.
